summary:
The Roman Empire (27 BC–476 AD in the West; 1453 AD in the East) was the most enduring and culturally influential political entity the Western world has ever produced — a successor to the Roman Republic that at its height governed some 70 million people across an area stretching from Scotland to Mesopotamia, from the Rhine to the Sahara. Its legacy of law, language, architecture, religion, and administrative organization continues to shape the modern world.

The Empire began with [[event:1083|Augustus - First Roman Emperor]]'s settlement of 27 BC, which ended the civil wars of the late Republic by establishing effective monarchy while maintaining republican forms. Augustus's successors — the Julio-Claudian, Flavian, Antonine, and Severan dynasties — extended Roman territory to its greatest extent under Trajan (r. 98–117 AD) while developing the administrative machinery of universal empire: a professional civil service, a standardized legal system, provincial governance, a road network of over 400,000 km, and urban infrastructure including aqueducts, sewers, baths, and public entertainment venues.

The [[event:1084|Pax Romana Begins]] — roughly two centuries of relative peace within Roman frontiers — allowed unprecedented economic and cultural development. Trade networks connected Britain to India via Roman-controlled ports; Greek culture in a Latinized form became the common currency of educated discourse; Roman citizenship was extended progressively until Caracalla's Edict of 212 AD granted it to virtually all free inhabitants of the Empire. The legal tradition Romans developed — property rights, contract law, the concept of legal personhood — became the foundation of European law.

Christianity's rise within the Empire was the single most consequential religious development of the ancient world. The new faith spread along Roman roads and trade routes, in the Greek koine language, using the communication infrastructure the Empire provided. After centuries of intermittent persecution, Constantine I's conversion in the early 4th century and the Edict of Milan (313 AD) transformed Christianity from a persecuted minority into the state religion, fundamentally altering the Empire's character and ensuring that Roman and Christian traditions would be transmitted together to medieval Europe.

The Empire's decline was gradual, complex, and has generated one of history's great historiographical debates — Gibbon's Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire inaugurated a tradition of analysis that continues today. Military pressure on multiple frontiers, economic disruption, political instability (the third century saw over fifty emperors in fifty years), and the transformation of Roman society by Christianity all contributed. The Western Empire ended conventionally in 476 AD when the Germanic chieftain Odoacer deposed the last western emperor; the Eastern Roman (Byzantine) Empire continued for nearly another thousand years until the Ottoman conquest of Constantinople in 1453.

The Empire's cultural legacy is immeasurable. Latin evolved into the Romance languages spoken by hundreds of millions today. Roman law underlies the legal systems of most of continental Europe and Latin America. Roman architecture provided the template for civic and religious building from the Renaissance through the present. The Roman Catholic Church, which preserved Latin literacy and Roman administrative traditions through the medieval period, directly inherited the Empire's institutional structures. The Roman Empire, more than any other historical entity, shaped the civilization that now dominates the globe.
